coredata.dc:title: Machine learning-assisted controllable preparation of multicolor phosphorescent carbon dots

coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Zihao
Li, Jiankang
Zhou, Sen
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) attracted significant attention due to their tunable emissions, abundant precursors, and high stability. However, the complex formation process, ambiguous structure and unclear luminescent mechanisms of phosphorescent CDs made their controllable preparation challenging, while the traditional methods failed to elucidate the relationship between the preparation conditions and photoluminescent properties. Herein, we integrated machine learning (ML) techniques into the research methodology to address these issues. To be specific, we constructed and optimized ML models that quantitatively revealed the connections between the reaction parameters and photoluminescent properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs. Leveraging the models, we achieved the efficient and controllable preparation of CDs with the desired properties such as specific emission wavelength. The successful ML-assisted customization of the emission wavelength of phosphorescent CDs was pivotal in enabling advanced applications such as advanced information encryption. These findings demonstrated the transformative potential of ML in guiding the precise preparation and expanding the practical applications of phosphorescent CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Machine learning
Controllable preparation
Multicolor-phosphorescence
Carbon dots
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Overall process of ML-assisted preparation for multicolor phosphorescent CDs
Preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs assisted by ML
Construction of the dataset and preliminary data analysis
Training, evaluation and optimization of ML models
Validation of ML model based on validation dataset
Prediction of preparing new phosphorescent CDs using ML model
Application of multicolor phosphorescent CDs in information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic description of HOMO-LUMO orbitals with increased carbonylation (a) and graphitization (b) through DFT calculations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Construction of ML models. (a) Pearson correlation coefficient heatmap of reaction parameters for CDs; Box plots of candidate models with evaluation metrics of R2 (b), MAE (c) and RMSE (d); Importance ranking of features including FL emission wavelength (e), RTP emission wavelength (f), FL Stokes shift (g) and RTP Stokes shift (h); Features: volume of added sulfuric acid (A), reaction time (B), volume of ethanol (C), volume of water (D). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Difference value between the predicted and experimental values for 42 kinds of CDs in validation dataset and 9 kinds of new CDs. FL emission wavelength (a), RTP emission wavelength (b), FL Stokes shift (c) and RTP Stokes shift (d) for 42 kinds of CDs; Corresponding scatter plots of predicted and experimental values (e), (f), (g) and (h) for 42 kinds of CDs; (i) FL emission wavelength, (j) RTP emission wavelength, (k) FL Stokes shift and (l) RTP Stokes shift for 9 kinds of new CDs; Corresponding 3D histograms of predicted and experimental values (m), (n), (o) and (p) for 9 kinds of new CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Application of multicolor phosphorescent CDs in information encryption. (a) Schematic illustration of binary encryption using CDs; (b) Construction of the HSV codebook; (c) Decryption process using the codebook. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Workflow of ML-assisted multicolor phosphorescent CDs preparation and applications. (a) Preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs using the microwave-assisted method; (b) Construction of ML models by training with input features and predicting the photoluminescent properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs; Applications of multicolor phosphorescent CDs in information encryption (c).

head.ce:title: Machine learning-assisted controllable preparation of multicolor phosphorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing, 401121, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) attracted significant attention due to their tunable emissions, abundant precursors, and high stability. However, the complex formation process, ambiguous structure and unclear luminescent mechanisms of phosphorescent CDs made their controllable preparation challenging, while the traditional methods failed to elucidate the relationship between the preparation conditions and photoluminescent properties. Herein, we integrated machine learning (ML) techniques into the research methodology to address these issues. To be specific, we constructed and optimized ML models that quantitatively revealed the connections between the reaction parameters and photoluminescent properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs. Leveraging the models, we achieved the efficient and controllable preparation of CDs with the desired properties such as specific emission wavelength. The successful ML-assisted customization of the emission wavelength of phosphorescent CDs was pivotal in enabling advanced applications such as advanced information encryption. These findings demonstrated the transformative potential of ML in guiding the precise preparation and expanding the practical applications of phosphorescent CDs.

Here we developed a machine learning-assisted strategy for preparing multicolor room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots. This method enabled the accurate prediction of their photoluminescent properties and supported their use in advanced information encryption and LED preparation.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, emerging as promising luminescent materials, have garnered significant attention due to their potential applications in multiple areas including bioimaging [1,2], anti-counterfeiting [3], information encryption [4], optoelectronic devices [5], and beyond. Nevertheless, traditional RTP materials are primarily composed of rare earth elements, precious metal complexes or pure organic compounds, which suffer from drawbacks such as biotoxicity [6], high cost [7] and complicated preparation procedures. In addition, due to the large nonradiative rate constant and weak spin-orbit coupling, the triplet excitons of traditional RTP materials are easily deactivated by nonradiative decay processes, leading to the relatively short lifetimes of RTP [8]. These inherent defects severely hinder their widespread applications [9]. Consequently, there is considerable scientific interest in developing RTP materials that are facile to prepare, low-cost, nontoxic and with tunable emission wavelength [10,11].

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging carbon material with a size below 10 nm, exhibiting a spherical or quasi-spherical shape [12] and have garnered increasing attentions owing to their low toxicity, biocompatibility, excellent photostability, cost-effectiveness and versatile luminescent properties [13]. Among them, CDs with RTP have attracted extensive attention based on their unique phosphorescent properties. The application scope of their RTP continues to expand, including but not limited to anti-counterfeiting [14,15], information encryption [16,17], sensing [18,19], optical device fabrication [20,21], and bioimaging [22,23]. Nonetheless, the inherently spin-forbidden intersystem crossing from the singlet to the triplet state, coupled with weak spin-orbit coupling, presents a significant barrier for CDs to achieve RTP. Based on the RTP mechanism, achieving the efficient RTP emission of CDs requires both an enhanced singlet-to-triplet intersystem crossing process and the suppression of nonradiative decay from the triplet state [24]. Towards this end, researchers raised several strategies to efficiently obtain CDs with RTP including hetero-/halogen-atom doping [25], crosslink-enhanced emission [26], aggregation-induced emission [27], polymer-doped system construction [28], host-guest interaction [29], covalent bond encapsulation [30] and crystal engineering [31]. However, the controllable preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs, remains challenging due to their complex structure and mechanism such as hydrogen bonds, covalent bonds, aggregation-induced effect, ionic bonds and halogen bonds [9]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a strategy that quantitatively elucidates the relationship between the reaction conditions and photoluminescence properties, while also providing an effective means to guide the controllable preparation of phosphorescent CDs without the extensive systematic characterization and analysis.

Currently, the traditional trial-and-error method still serves as a primary approach for obtaining target CDs. However, such a method inherently carries subjectivity, randomness or unpredictability coupled with inefficiency, leading to the difficulty of obtaining satisfactory CDs. Therefore, it is crucial to develop new strategies towards efficient and accurate preparation of target CDs. In recent years, machine learning (ML), as a branch of artificial intelligence (AI), develops rapidly with advancement of computer hardware and software [32]. ML has been widely applied in various research disciplines [33,34], and it could be utilized to construct models and achieve predictions based on acquired abundant data [35], even without a complete understanding of “black box” algorithms. These capabilities facilitated successful applications of ML in fields of chemistry and biology, such as spectroscopy prediction [36], cancer biomarker detection [37], biosensor construction [38], and chemical synthesis [39]. However, guiding the preparation and application of CDs by introducing ML is still in its infancy.

Actually, ML have been employed to guide the preparation process of fluorescent CDs. The process of CDs is a complex and time-consuming scientific process, which may be influenced by various factors such as specific methodologies or conditions. ML facilitates the discovery of relationships between the preparation process and the intrinsic properties of CDs through iterative analysis of experimental parameters. For example, Chen et al. successfully employed ML to guide the preparation of multicolor fluorescent CDs and investigate the relationship between the fluorescence (FL) and reaction conditions [40]. Their findings revealed that the solvent's boiling point exerted the most significant impact on the emission wavelength, while both the precursor ratio and the boiling point predominantly influenced the quantum yields (QY). Furthermore, Han et al. utilized ML to optimize the QY of CDs, which reached up to 39.3 % [41]. In addition, Senanayake et al. successfully predicted the fluorescent color and emission wavelength of CDs, achieving a prediction accuracy of 94 % with the assistance of ML [42].

In addition to guiding the preparation process of CDs, the ML-assisted strategy for CDs was effectively employed to classify and differentiate various analytes [43,44], LED assembly [45] and antibacterial applications [46]. Nevertheless, the application of the ML-assisted strategy in the field of phosphorescent CDs was rarely reported. Muyassiroh et al. used ML to enhance the phosphorescent lifetime of single-color phosphorescent CDs [47]. However, the research through the ML-assisted strategy for guiding the controllable preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs, as well as elucidating the inherent relationship between reaction conditions and phosphorescent properties, remained unreported and challenging, indicating its significant research value.

Based on the current research of ML strategy in the field of CDs and our previous study [48], we introduced an ML-assisted strategy for the preparation process of multicolor phosphorescent CDs. In our previous study, the extensive characterization and theoretical analysis only provided the qualitative insights into the relationship between the preparation conditions and photoluminescent properties of the multicolor phosphorescent CDs, which did not achieve the accurate prediction or control. The current study introduced an ML-assisted strategy to quantitatively analyze these intrinsic relationships, accurately predict their photoluminescent properties across a broader spectral range, and guide the controllable preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs without the necessary and extensive characterization or theoretical analysis. Through this approach, the limitations of the previous study were complemented, and the application of ML for preparing and regulating the phosphorescent CDs was effectively expanded. Considering the advantage of the predictive power by ML and expanding the practical applications of multicolor RTP, we further developed an encryption method using the HSV color model. Compared with the previous work, this HSV-based encryption demonstrated the superior performance to encrypt information and enabled the more effective utilization of multicolor RTP.

Dicyandiamide (DCD) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while the solvent of ethanol was acquired from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was obtained from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chongqing, China), and the silicone adhesive and InGaN chips were from Zhanwang Long Technology Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Meanwhile, all the commercial reagents were employed directly without the subsequent purification, and the ultrapure water generated by an Aquapro AWL-0502-P water system (resistivity: 18.25 MΩ cm) were utilized during the experimental procedures.
All the fluorescent and phosphorescent analyses were achieved by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan).

CDs discussed here were prepared using the microwave-assisted method. To be specific, 1.0 g of DCD was parallelly placed into individual crucible, and various solutions with different volume ratios (H2O to ethanol: 4:0, 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3) were introduced to form 6 groups, and sulfuric acid with different volumes (200 μL, 350 μL, 500 μL, 650 μL, 800 μL) was respectively added into each previous group with gentle stirring. The obtained mixtures of 30 combinations were then subjected to microwave irradiation for various durations (ranging from 70 s to 130 s), thus 210 kinds of CDs were acquired.

All theoretical calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 software. Within the framework of density functional theory (DFT), the B3LYP/6-31G × method was employed to fully optimize all molecular structures, thus providing the HOMO-LUMO band gaps and visual orbitals of the target structures.

Typically, one kind of phosphorescent CDs previously prepared was selected to design the binary encryption. In brief, the phosphorescent CDs and fluorescent CDs were respectively mixed with an organic silicone gel and coated onto the black cardstock followed by the exposure with 365 nm of ultraviolet. After switching off the ultraviolet, a binary-based codebook was constructed according to the present or absent of RTP with the output of 1 or 0.

Furthermore, ten kinds of CDs exhibiting diverse FL and RTP were selected, and their fluorescent and afterglow images with 365 nm of ultraviolet on or off were captured. Through the graphics operation by the software of OpenCV, the HSV values of individual CDs were extracted, thus building up a codebook.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall process of ML-assisted preparation for multicolor phosphorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To integrate ML into the controllable preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs, we carried out the following work. To begin with, we utilized the microwave-assisted method to prepare multicolor phosphorescent CDs (Scheme 1 a). Subsequently, a dataset for training and optimizing ML models was constructed based on the data previously obtained. With the aid of the optimized models, we successfully predicted the photoluminescent properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs with high accuracy (Scheme 1b), and further elucidate the correlation between the reaction parameters and photoluminescent properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs in depth. Next, we applied the models to efficiently prepare the multicolor phosphorescent CDs with certain properties according to the application requirements for information encryption (Scheme 1c). In general, these results suggested that leveraging the powerful analytical and computational capability of ML could assist in gaining the deeper insights between the reaction parameters and photoluminescent properties, thus efficiently obtaining the customized phosphorescent CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs assisted by ML
ce:section.ce:para: In our previous work, we have employed dicyandiamide (DCD) as a precursor to prepare a series of multicolor phosphorescent CDs [48], and three specific CDs selected have been systematically characterized and investigated as reported. Accordingly, the current research avoided redundant characterizations. Instead, we closely focused on obtaining dataset and constructing ML models towards predicting their fluorescent and phosphorescent properties of the multicolor phosphorescent CDs.

We initially identified four kernel parameters of preparing CDs as input data from previous experiments, and these parameters consisted of reaction time, volume of sulfuric acid, water and ethanol (Table S1). Based on our previous findings, using DCD as a precursor, employing sulfuric acid and regulating the volume ratio of ethanol allowed us to prepare a series of CDs with varying degrees of graphitization and carbonyl content, and these CDs exhibited diverse fluorescent and phosphorescent emission wavelength.

Therefore, we conducted preparations with 210 different parameter combinations and identified their photoluminescent properties including emission wavelength and Stokes shift. Based on the data obtained, we thereby constructed a dataset that was randomly shuffled and divided into training and validation sets at a ratio of 8:2, and these sets were further utilized for training models and preliminarily evaluating models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the dataset and preliminary data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: During our previous report, multicolor phosphorescent CDs with various degrees of graphitization and carbonylation were prepared by regulating the volume of sulfuric acid and ethanol [48]. Specifically, the increased carbonylation and graphitization enlarged the sp2 conjugated domain and reduced the bandgap of CDs, consequently inducing the redshift of emission wavelength. To validate this conclusion, we conducted corresponding DFT calculations based on the constructed molecular structures. As the carbonylation and graphitization of these molecules increasing, the conjugated domains accordingly expanded, leading to reduced bandgaps of HOMO-LUMO (Fig. 1 a and b). These results further confirmed our previous conclusion, and thus, we employed these critical parameters to construct the following datasets.

To establish the dataset for training ML models, we hereby prepared 210 kinds of CDs and recorded their emission wavelength and Stokes shift of both FL and RTP (Fig. S1–S4). Meanwhile, emission wavelength distribution of FL and RTP for these multicolor phosphorescent CDs were recorded (Fig. S5a and b) and exhibited similar variation trends. Also, FL and RTP Stokes shift of CDs similarly displayed the intermediate-high, two-end low pattern (Fig. S5c and d). Through the preliminary data analysis, we determined that the volumes of sulfuric acid and ethanol exerted the most significant influence on the photoluminescent properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs among four reaction parameters, which aligned with our previous work. An intuitive examination of the data distribution further revealed that increasing the amount of sulfuric acid and the ethanol ratio led to a gradual red-shift in the emission wavelength of CDs. Building on our experimental experience and theoretical foundation, we understood that adjusting these parameters modulated the structural features of the CDs-specifically, the degree of graphitization and the carbonyl content [48]. Specifically, increasing the ethanol fraction lowered the boiling point of the reaction solvent, which enhanced carbonization and dehydration processes, while the dehydrating and oxidizing activities of H2SO4 promoted the higher graphitization, larger particle size and increased carbonyl content. The synergistic effects of increased carbonyl content and extended sp2-conjugated domains (graphitization) collectively narrowed the band gap, and facilitated the efficient intersystem crossing and the generation of triplet-state excitons, resulting in effective RTP and red-shifts of the emission wavelength. Therefore, by adjusting the ethanol fraction and the amount of sulfuric acid in the reaction system, the CDs with varying degrees of carbonylation and graphitization could be obtained, thus achieving the multicolor FL and RTP.

The intuitive analysis of the data allows for a certain level of perception regarding the correlation between reaction parameters and photoluminescent properties, however, it lacks precision. To avoid that, we thus fully utilized the data analysis and processing capability of ML to deeply elucidate the correlation between reaction parameters and photoluminescent properties. Initially, Pearson correlation coefficients (PCC) among the four parameters were evaluated, and the corresponding heatmap was shown in Fig. 2 a. Typically, the PCC values, which range from −1 to 1, quantify the degree of linear correlation between features. A PCC of 1 indicates a perfect linear correlation (complete redundancy), while a PCC of 0 suggests no linear correlation, indicating that the features are independent. Ideally, the PCC between the selected features should be far from 1 to ensure independence. The results demonstrated the absence of strong correlations among these parameters, satisfying the requirement for independence. Therefore, these four parameters could serve as input features of ML models.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Training, evaluation and optimization of ML models
ce:section.ce:para: Towards harvesting the favorable learning and predictive capability of the constructed model, we evaluated the performance of several output models using the prepared dataset, and subsequently selected the models with the optimal predictive ability. Initially, we randomized the constructed dataset and divided it as training and validation sets with a ratio of 8:2. Next, the training set was utilized for training ML models, while the validation set for evaluating model performance, and further, the optimization of model parameters was accomplished by virtue of grid search and learning curve. For model comparison, we employed Decision Trees (DT), Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) as four candidate models. To visualize the results, the data of model selection were described using box plots (Fig. 2b, c, and d), and the performance of the ML models was evaluated using three key metrics: the coefficient of determination (R2), mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE). R2 serves as a crucial indicator of how effectively the features explain the variability in the target variable, with values closer to 1 reflecting a better model fit. In contrast, MAE and RMSE measure the difference between predicted and actual values, where the lower values denote more precise predictions. Based on the median line distributions of the four models, the XGBoost model consistently delivered the higher accuracy and robustness, exhibiting the best overall performance. To be specific, the XGBoost model exhibited the highest R2 value and the lowest RMSE and MAE values. Considering the generalization capacity and fitting degree, we thus selected XGBoost as the optimal ML model during subsequent investigation and fully optimized while ensuring no overfitting and the detailed performance metrics are shown in Table S2.

It was challengeable to exactly determine the impact of each reaction parameter on their photoluminescent properties of CDs through visualized analysis. Hence, we introduced the algorithm of importance ranking to calculate the importance of features (Fig. 2e, f, g and h). To be specific, the algorithm converts the contribution of all features to the target property into values summing to 1, where a higher value indicates a greater impact of the feature on the target property. For the FL emission wavelength and FL Stokes shift of phosphorescent CDs, the contribution values of sulfuric acid volume and ethanol volume exceeded 0.7, while the contribution of water volume remained below 0.2, and that of reaction time was under 0.1. Regarding the RTP emission wavelength and RTP Stokes shift, the ethanol volume contributed more than 0.6, playing a dominant role. On the whole, the results revealed that volumes of sulfuric acid and ethanol served as the major factors that influenced the maximum emission wavelength and Stokes shift of CDs, while the reaction time exhibited relatively weak impact. These findings agreed with our previous conclusion, where both sulfuric acid and ethanol played the pivotal roles during the preparations of multicolor phosphorescent CDs. These results highlighted that the algorithm of importance ranking offered an effective way to explore the significance of various reaction features towards preparing CDs. Consequently, ML models provided the possibility of selectively preparing target CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of ML model based on validation dataset
ce:section.ce:para: When XGBoost was confirmed as the optimal model, we further validated its ability of predicting four photoluminescent properties based on validation dataset. Briefly, we compared the predicted values with the experimental values to validate model performance. The results revealed that the maximum FL and RTP emission wavelength experimentally obtained approached to the predicted values (Fig. 3 a and b), and the distribution of predicted (y) and experimental values (x) aligned along the curve of y = x (Fig. 3e and f), reinforcing the previous conclusion. Similar as previously predicting the maximum emission wavelength of CDs by XGBoost, we thereby validated the capacity of XGBoost predicting the corresponding Stokes shift. The results also demonstrated that the predicted and experimental values for FL and RTP Stokes shift were consistent with each other (Fig. 3c and d), and uniformly distributed along the curve of y = x (Fig. 3g and h). The models achieved the high R2 values of 0.95 for FL emission wavelength, 0.94 for RTP emission wavelength, 0.89 for FL Stokes shift and 0.94 for RTP Stokes shift, revealing a strong correlation between predicted and actual values. Furthermore, the low MAE values (5.77 nm for FL emission, 5.17 nm for RTP emission, 3.51 nm for FL Stokes shift and 4.66 nm for RTP Stokes shift) and RMSE values (7.85 nm, 7.04 nm, 5.02 nm and 6.66 nm, respectively) confirmed the model's accuracy and precision. Overall, these metrics clearly demonstrated that the XGBoost model showed the robust performance on the validation dataset, accurately predicted the photoluminescent properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs without overfitting, and thus met the requirements of reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction of preparing new phosphorescent CDs using ML model
ce:section.ce:para: Under some circumstances, certain ML models may exhibit high predictive accuracy on both the training and validation datasets, but fail to precisely predict the outcomes with unknown parameters. Therefore, we systematically varied the preparation parameters to obtain nine types of phosphorescent CDs, each exhibiting distinct photoluminescent properties that aligned with the requirements of subsequent applications. The emission wavelengths and Stokes shifts were recorded (Table S3) along with the specific preparation conditions (Table S4), ensuring the coherence of our experimental design and enabling the evaluation of the ML model's generalization capability. In addition, 3D FL and RTP waterfall plots of 9 kinds of phosphorescent CDs were obtained, exhibiting the excitation-dependent characteristic (Figure S6 and Figure S7). We also employed XGBoost to predict their photoluminescent properties of these CDs, and the results revealed a favorable agreement between the experimental and predicted values (Fig. 3i–p). The performance metrics for the XGBoost models in predicting nine kinds of new CDs clearly indicated that the models achieved the high predictive accuracy for phosphorescent CDs preparation under unknown conditions (Table S5). Specifically, the model achieved an R2 of 0.97 for the FL emission wavelength and 0.94 for the RTP emission wavelength, indicating an excellent correlation between predicted and experimental values. Additionally, the RTP Stokes shift yielded an R2 of 0.95, further confirming the model's precision in capturing the key photoluminescent properties. Although the R2 for the FL Stokes shift was slightly lower at 0.81, it still reflected a high level of predictive accuracy. Moreover, the MAE values, ranging from 4.23 nm to 6.14 nm, and the RMSE values, between 4.81 nm and 8.33 nm, underscored the model's low error margins. Overall, these metrics validated that the XGBoost models reliably predicted photoluminescent properties under the novel preparation conditions, fully meeting our research requirements.

Taken together, the optimized ML models not only demonstrated the excellent performance on the validation dataset but also accurately predicted four key photoluminescent properties of CDs under novel preparation conditions. These results confirmed that the models met the experimental requirements and was highly effective in guiding the controllable preparation of multicolor phosphorescent CDs with customized properties. Unlike the previous studies that focused on enhancing a specific performance parameter such as QY, our approach was designed to capture a broad range of the preparation parameters that reflected diverse application requirements. Rather than pinpointing a singular optimal reaction condition, our strategy customized the emission wavelength of phosphorescent CDs to meet various practical demands. This flexibility not only minimized the trial-and-error process in the laboratory, thereby enhancing experimental efficiency, but also raised new possibilities for customizing CDs for a wide array of applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of multicolor phosphorescent CDs in information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: Due to their tunable phosphorescent properties, low-cost and high stability, room-temperature phosphorescent CDs were extensively applied for information encryption [9,49]. Initially, we selected two kinds of CDs without or with RTP for binary information encryption, and the phosphorescent CDs were obtained assisted by ML model. In brief, the output signals were regulated by the presence or absence of RTP, while RTP output the signal of 1 as well as its absence for 0. This 5-bit binary code corresponded to 26 letters that formed a codebook (Table S6), and the decryption process for the word of CARBON was depicted (Fig. 4 a). Additionally, we designed an encryption strategy by leveraging the HSV color model alongside our pre-trained ML models to achieve advanced information encryption with various phosphorescent CDs. The predictive power of the XGBoost models allowed us to accurately customize the multicolor emission properties of multicolor phosphorescent CDs to meet diverse application. The HSV color model was deliberately selected for two key reasons: firstly, its intuitive color representation closely mimics human visual perception, and secondly, this color space conversion facilitates more flexible and dynamic encryption strategies. By effectively leveraging the comprehensive photoluminescent data predicted by the XGBoost model, this approach significantly enhanced both the functionality and practical applicability of our encryption system. To be specific, ten kinds of CDs with diverse colors were prepared by virtue of the ML model we proposed, and corresponding fluorescent and phosphorescent images were captured with 365 nm of irradiation on or off. These images were input into the HSV model to extract HSV values of their FL and RTP, which were used to construct a codebook (Fig. 4b). By analyzing the difference of HSV values according to the codebook, we could decode the encrypted information (Fig. 4c). Here, we designed two encryption strategies by multicolor phosphorescent CDs, further demonstrating the value of ML model assisted the preparation of the target phosphorescent CDs.

In summary, this study built upon our previous work and involved preparation of 210 multicolor phosphorescent CDs. We systematically collected and integrated various reaction parameters and photoluminescent properties to construct multiple datasets, thus successfully establishing a ML-based framework. Specifically, the XGBoost model, after extensive training, filtering and optimization, achieved the high predictive accuracy-with R2 values reaching up to 0.9 and low MAE and RMSE-and demonstrated the robust generalization across both the validation dataset and novel preparation conditions. By employing an importance ranking algorithm, we elucidated the intrinsic relationship between the preparation parameters and photoluminescent properties, thereby enabling the controllable preparation of CDs. Furthermore, our work designed two advanced strategies for information encryption using the prepared multicolor phosphorescent CDs, showcasing the practical applications of our approach in the fields of advanced information security. While the results are promising, we acknowledge the limitations of the current ML models, including potential challenges with overfitting in certain parameter spaces and the need for further refinement to incorporate additional precursors and unknown variables. Addressing these limitations in future research will enhance the robustness and broader applicability of ML-assisted preparation, ultimately contributing to the more efficient and cost-effective material preparation.

Zihao Yan: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiankang Li: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sen Zhou: Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Xiaoming Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
Data will be made available on reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (CSTB2024NSCQ-MSX0417), SAMR Science and Technology Program (2023MK102), Chongqing Scientific Institution Incentive Performance Guiding Special Projects (CSTB2023JXJL-YFX0027), Chongqing Municipal Market Supervision Administration Science and Technology Program (CQSJKJDW2023005).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2025.120394.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic room-temperature phosphorescence materials for bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, H. Li, M. Yang, W. Dai, J. Shi, B. Tong, Z. Cai, Z. Wang, Y. Dong, X. Yu, Organic room-temperature phosphorescence materials for bioimaging, Chem. Commun. 59(36) (2023) 5329-5342. https://doi.org/10.1039/D3CC00923H.

Interfaces 11(20) (2019) 18103-18110. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.9b01615.
